result: The moon is teaching me patience tonight. It hangs there like a silver coin pressed against the dark thumbprint of sky, refusing to hurry its arc across the roofline. Somewhere in the neighbor’s yard, crickets have broken their winter silence and started stitching sound back into the world, each chirp a tiny knot tying tonight to every other April I’ve lived through. I keep thinking memory is a place I can visit, like a pocket park tucked between office buildings, but it’s more like a tide that brings debris and carries off what I thought I’d anchored. My mother’s laugh surfaces first—bright as carnival glass—then the smell of her carnations, then the way she used to hum while folding laundry, as if cotton held a melody. I try to line these fragments up like dominoes, but they topple into each other, becoming something new: a version of her that never existed yet feels truer than photographs. Across the street, the porch light clicks off; someone else has decided the dark is safe enough. I stay outside, barefoot on cool planks, letting the minutes pool around my ankles. Somewhere inside, my phone buzzes with headlines that will be obsolete by morning, but out here the only breaking news is the way the wind rearranges the lilac shadows. I breathe it in until my ribs feel porous, until the moon finally slips behind the maple, a slow coin swallowed by the slot of tomorrow. When I go in, I leave the door cracked, just in case patience wants to follow.

Somewhere inside, my phone buzzes with headlines that will be obsolete by morning, but out here the only breaking news is the way the wind rearranges the lilac shadows. prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
